summary: Gruber revisits a two-year-old piece by Max Read documenting the history of fake text messages in Apple's marketing materials. While praising Read's thorough research and acknowledging the piece is genuinely fun, Gruber takes issue with its cynical tone, arguing that of course Apple's marketing texts are idealized and phony — that's the entire point of marketing screenshots. Gruber finds the more interesting observation is how much craft Apple puts into making these fake texts convincingly aspirational without being distracting. He traces Read's reflexive cynicism to his background as editor-in-chief of Gawker, a publication whose defining attitude was that nothing deserved anything but withering scorn. The result, Gruber argues, is an otherwise excellent piece undermined by an unnecessary aftertaste of contempt.
key_points: Apple's marketing screenshots feature obviously idealized fake texts, but that's exactly what marketing materials should be — the alternative would be bizarre
What's genuinely interesting is the craft Apple puts into these fake texts: they're 'good phony,' pitch-perfect for the brand without distracting from the keynote
The fake texts serve the opposite purpose of Liquid Glass: they're fake content designed to emphasize real software
Read's Gawker background explains his reflexive cynicism — Gawker's ethos held that no subject deserved anything but withering scorn
Read previously had an Apple TV+ show killed by Tim Cook personally due to its Gawker-based subject matter, though Gruber doesn't attribute the cynicism to personal grudge
money_quotes: What other kinds of fictional people are going to be portrayed by Apple in their marketing screenshots? Ugly unhappy illiterates who take bad photos and never go anywhere?
They're good phony. Pitch-perfect for Apple's Designed-in-California brand.
The purpose of these fake texts is the opposite of the supposed intention of the Liquid Glass design language: it's fake content meant to put the emphasis on the real software.
The withering cynicism of his tone is at odds with the fact that he took the time to document their history so thoroughly.
Gawker was infused by the sort of attitude that holds all marketing in contempt.
The Gawker attitude was that no subject was worthy of anything but withering scorn. I never could abide that.
It's like an otherwise delightful cocktail with one distinctive unpleasant ingredient, which ingredient was added, deliberately, to imbue the libation with an aftertaste of spite.
key_insight: Apple's fake marketing texts are genuinely interesting artifacts of corporate craft, and the real story is the care that goes into them — not the obvious fact that marketing materials are idealized.
